title: Classic chocolate brownies
subtitle: Dense, fudgy chocolate squares
excerpt: A no-frills fudgy brownie: dark chocolate and butter melted together, lots of sugar, a small amount of flour to give structure without drying the crumb. The result is dense and squat, not cakey.
sourceNotes: The chocolate brownie is an American development of the late nineteenth century. The first published recipes appear in American domestic manuals from the 1890s; Fannie Farmer's Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896 edition) and the Sears Roebuck catalogue (1897) both carry early brownie recipes. The fudgy, flour-minimal form became the American standard through the early twentieth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze individual squares. Defrost at room temperature; the texture is very good from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Brownies are better the day after baking once the fudgy centre has set. Keep covered at room temperature.

content.text: The difference between fudgy and cakey brownies is flour: this recipe uses 80 g for a 20 x 30 cm tin. That is deliberately low. The batter bakes up dense, set on the edges with a slight wobble in the centre at 25 minutes; that wobble firms into the fudgy texture as the tin cools. Cutting too soon (while still warm) gives a messy slice. An hour in the tin after baking, then an hour in the fridge, gives clean squares.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Line a 20 x 30 cm rectangular tin with baking paper. Melt the chocolate and butter together in a medium saucepan over very low heat, stirring regularly. Once smooth, remove from the heat and leave to cool for 5 minutes.

Whisk the sugar into the melted chocolate mixture. Add the eggs one at a time, whisking well after each. Stir in the vanilla. Sift over the flour, cocoa, and salt and fold in with a spatula until just combined. Pour into the prepared tin and level. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes. The edges should be set and the centre should have a slight wobble when the tin is gently shaken. A skewer inserted 3 cm from the edge should come out clean; a skewer through the centre may have a few moist crumbs, which is correct. Cool completely in the tin, then refrigerate for at least 1 hour before cutting into squares.
